raw_consumer_statement:
Citibank failed to overturn a fraudulent/misrepresented charge for auto repair, detailed below : I took my vehicle to an auto shop for a timing chain replacement in late XX/XX/year>. The vehicle had new engine noise present immediately upon receipt from the shop, which I documented in a video. I attempted to resolve the issue with the vendor with numerous phone calls and a text message exchange where I sent them the video of the engine noise ( the vendor confirmed receipt ), but they would not reinspect their repair under warranty and instead wanted to charge me additional diagnostic fees. 

After taking the vehicle to a second shop for independent diagnosis, it was discovered that the vendor had failed to replace a critical component in the job ( timing chain tensioner ), which not only caused the new engine noise, but also caused their repair to fail immediately. This was also despite billing me for an entire " XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX XXXX' on their invoice. The Technical Product Director for XXXX also confirmed in an email that these components were not theirs and showed photos of the correct components with their company 's distinctive manufacturer 's tooling as proof. I ultimately had to take the vehicle back to the second shop and pay for a second repair to correct the first vendor 's error. 

Despite this overwhelming proof that the vendor ; a ) failed to fulfill their contracted scope of work entirely, b ) refused to fix their mistake despite numerous offers to do so, and c ) intentionally misrepresented their repair by billing me for parts that they did not install, my bank ( Citibank ) decided not to uphold my dispute with the vendor and kept the charge on my account.
